title: Doria canadensis
taxonomy: Doria canadensis is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Boraginaceae. It is a member of the genus Doria, which is part of the larger group of plants known as the borage family. This classification is based on the plant's characteristics, such as its leaf shape and flower structure. The family Boraginaceae includes over 2,000 species of flowering plants, many of which are found in temperate and subtropical regions. Doria canadensis is native to eastern North America.
uses: Doria canadensis, also known as Eastern Bladderwort, is a carnivorous plant that has been used in traditional medicine for its purported health benefits. The plant's leaves and stems are said to be used to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. Its medicinal properties are attributed to the presence of alkaloids and other compounds that may have antibacterial and anti-inflammatory effects. However, further research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using Doria canadensis for medicinal purposes. As a result, it is essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using the plant for therapeutic purposes.

properties: Doria canadensis, also known as American bugleweed or water bugle, has been used in traditional medicine for its therapeutic properties. constituents: Doria canadensis is also known as American water hemlock, and its active constituents include alkaloids such as cicutoxin, cicutol, and others. These compounds are responsible for the plant's toxic properties. Cicutoxin, in particular, is a powerful neurotoxin that can cause seizures, muscle spasms, and other systemic effects. Cicutol is also toxic, but its effects are less well understood. The combination of these and other compounds makes Doria canadensis one of the most toxic plants in North America.

side_effects: Doria canadensis is also known as Indian plum or thimbleberry, and its berries have been used in traditional medicine for various purposes.
